metadata.working_title: Sex-specific resilience of neocortex to food restriction

abstract_sketch.problem: Mammals evolved sex-specific adaptations to reduce energy usage when food is scarce; the brain is energetically expensive but sex-specific cortical adaptations remain poorly characterized.
abstract_sketch.gap: Whether and how the neocortex adapts its energy usage and function to food restriction in a sex-specific manner is unknown.
abstract_sketch.approach: Moderate food restriction (85% free-feeding bodyweight, 2-3 weeks) in adult male and female mice combined with AMPK Thr172 phosphorylation assays, PPARalpha DNA-binding assays, V1 RNA-seq with DESeq2 and GSEA, two-photon ATeam1.03YEMK FRET-based ATP imaging, GCaMP6s calcium imaging of orientation-selective V1 neurons, and Bayes factor analysis.
abstract_sketch.key_results: To lose 15% bodyweight, females required a 40% greater food restriction (39% of ad libitum) than males (27% of ad libitum). Serum leptin dropped 72% in males (6.45 vs 1.84 ng/mL; t=4.58, df=66, p<0.0001) but only 28% non-significantly in females (3.94 vs 2.83 ng/mL; t=1.00, df=66, p=0.32). V1 AMPK Thr172 phosphorylation increased 2.9-fold in males (p=0.022) vs 1.4-fold non-significantly in females (p=0.11). Food restriction altered 657 transcripts in males vs 585 in females with 125 shared, and regulated 34 vs 14 GSEA gene sets respectively. Cortical ATP half-time slowed 24% in males (8.48 to 10.51 min; p=0.0067) but only 12% non-significantly in females (p=0.18). V1 orientation selectivity dropped 27% in males (0.58 to 0.42; p=0.0009) vs 13% non-significantly in females (p=0.08).
abstract_sketch.impact: The neocortex contributes to sex-specific energy-saving adaptations under metabolic challenge; dietary manipulations used to motivate animals in behavioural studies likely affect males and females differently.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Mammals adapt energy usage to food scarcity in sex-specific ways (peripheral tissue).
Females preserve fat mass and bodyweight better than males during food restriction; reproductive tissue losses are more pronounced in females.
The brain consumes 20% of caloric intake in humans (cortex > half) yet cortical energy adaptations are poorly understood.
Prior work in male mice: food restriction reduces V1 ATP usage and orientation selectivity through decreased serum leptin.
Gap: whether females show similar cortical adaptations is unknown.
Contribution: we test V1 energy usage and visual coding in adult male and female mice under 2-3 weeks of 15% bodyweight loss using molecular, transcriptomic, and two-photon imaging approaches.

subsections.title: Sex-specific impact of food restriction on weight loss and serum leptin
content_bullets: Baseline: males 27.82 g vs females 23.21 g; same daily ad libitum intake despite 80% body mass ratio.
Achieving 15% bodyweight loss required 39% of ad libitum intake in females vs 27% in males (40% greater restriction in females).
Serum leptin -72% in males (6.45 -> 1.84 ng/mL; p<0.0001) but only -28% in females (3.94 -> 2.83 ng/mL; p=0.32).
Figure 1 reports weight trajectories, daily intake, bodyweight at harvest, serum leptin, AMPK and PPARalpha.

subsections.title: Cellular energy-regulating pathways in V1 are significantly impacted by food restriction in males but not females
content_bullets: AMPK Thr172 phosphorylation: males 2.9x (13.19 -> 38.21 AU/ug; p=0.022); females 1.4x, n.s. (20.94 -> 29.52 AU/ug; p=0.11).
PPARalpha DNA binding in V1: significantly increased in males (p=0.013), unchanged in females (p=0.99).
RNA-seq DESeq2 on n=4/group: 657 DE genes in males, 585 in females at p-adj<0.1 with 125 shared.
GSEA Hallmark: 34 gene sets in males vs 14 in females at p-adj<0.05 with 13 shared.
Oxidative phosphorylation, mTORC1, fatty acid metabolism, PI3K/AKT/mTOR significantly enriched in males; estrogen response and inflammation pathways also male-selective.

subsections.title: ATP usage in V1 is significantly decreased under food restriction in males but not females
content_bullets: Two-photon ATeam1.03YEMK FRET imaging with focal ATP synthesis inhibitors during natural-scene visual stimulation.
Half-time of FRET decay in males: 8.48 -> 10.51 min (24% decrease; t=2.87, df=37, p=0.0067; n=11 CTR, 10 FR).
Half-time of FRET decay in females: 8.66 -> 9.66 min (12% decrease; t=1.36, df=37, p=0.18; n=12 CTR, 8 FR).
Darkness (Supplemental Figure 1): no effect in either sex (males p=0.89; females p=0.52).

subsections.title: Orientation selectivity in V1 is significantly reduced by food restriction in males but not females
content_bullets: Two-photon GCaMP6s imaging of V1 layer 2/3 neurons during drifting gratings.
OSI in females 0.60 -> 0.52 (13% decrease; t=1.90, df=27, p=0.08; n=7/9).
DSI (Suppl Fig 3): males p=0.023 (t=2.16, df=12, n=6/8); females p=0.16 (t=1.49, df=14, n=7/9).
Pupil diameter (Suppl Fig 2) not different across sex/diet (pooled male vs female p=0.42).

subsections.title: Bayes factor analysis confirms sex-specific metabolic stress impact
content_bullets: BF10 several-fold greater for males than females across all parameters.
Only 3/10 female parameters had BF10 < 0.33 (substantive null evidence) - suggests residual effects below statistical detection.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Summary: V1 energy usage and coding are resilient to food restriction in females; robustly reduced in males.
Role of leptin and sex hormones: estrogen suppresses intake, augments leptin-STAT3 signaling, boosts mitochondrial function.
Sex-specific AMPK/mTOR regulation extends prior peripheral findings to neocortex.
Implications for food-restriction motivation protocols in cortical neuroscience studies.
Relevance to dietary interventions for neurological disease (epilepsy, AD).
Limitations: single cortical area, one restriction protocol, young adults only, power limits direct sex x diet interaction tests.

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Animals 7-9 weeks, ad libitum (CTR) vs 85% free-feeding bodyweight (FR) for 2-3 weeks; animals sated before recordings.
Serum leptin assay (n details per group).
AMPK Thr172 phosphorylation normalized to total AMPK in V1 tissue.
PPARalpha DNA binding normalized to protein level.
RNA-seq on V1 tissue; DESeq2 differential expression p-adj<0.1; GSEA Hallmark p-adj<0.05.
Two-photon imaging in awake head-fixed mice; Thy1-ATeam1.03YEMK FRET with focal ATP synthesis inhibitors during natural scene stimulation and in darkness.
GCaMP6s layer 2/3 imaging with drifting gratings, OSI and DSI computed.
Two-way ANOVAs; Bayes factor analysis (BF10) with effect sizes and 95% CIs.
